---
title: "Research"
pagetitle: "Research — Bingjin Xue"
---

## Published and Forthcoming

::: {.paper}
[Destination Crisis and Economic Ripple Effects in Platform-Mediated Tourism Markets]{.title}

[with Hanna Lee, Yifei Wang, and Jinwon Kim]{.authors}

[*Journal of Hospitality and Tourism Management*, 2026, 67, 101437.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Can a single act of violence disrupt an entire regional tourism market? We use the 2018 Parkland school shooting as a localized safety shock and a triple-difference design on 419,057 Florida Airbnb listings to trace how perceived safety propagates through supply, demand, and host listing behavior in platform-mediated tourism markets.

</details>

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhtm.2026.101437)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Bittersweet: Grandparenting and Elderly Mental Health in the Two-Child Policy Era]{.title}

[with Dapeng Chen and Shin-Yi Chou]{.authors}

[*Journal of Development Economics*, 2025, 103670.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Does grandparenting improve or erode elderly mental health? Using China's 2016 universal two-child policy as a shock to grandchild arrivals, we find that the resulting rise in grandparental involvement raises CESD-10 depression scores by 6.8% and the frequency of symptoms — restless sleep, irritability — by 8.9% to 14.3%. Effects concentrate in urban grandparents and in paternal grandparents, especially paternal grandmothers. We find no evidence of elevated severe clinical depression.

</details>

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jdeveco.2025.103670)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Implications of the Decline in LGBT Rights for Population Mental Health: Evidence from Polish "LGBT-free Zones"]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer and Anna Poznańska]{.authors}

[*Journal of Health Economics*, 2025, 100, 102973.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do anti-LGBT policies harm population mental health? Using Polish "LGBT-free zone" adoptions in 2019–2020 and a difference-in-differences design against bordering non-adopting counties, we find suicide attempts rose 16.5%, suicide deaths rose 17.0%, and deaths from external causes rose 10.6%. The rise in attempts concentrates among ages 13–18 and 45–49, and coincides with an 8.8% increase in prescription sales for addictive-disorder drugs.

</details>

<div class="actions">
[Published version](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhealeco.2025.102973) ·
[NBER version](https://www.nber.org/papers/w31702)
</div>
:::

## Under Review

::: {.paper}
[Abortion, Economic Hardship, and Crime]{.title}

[with Erkmen G. Aslim, Wei Fu, Caitlin Myers, and Erdal Tekin]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag} NBER Working Paper 34245]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do abortion restrictions fuel financially motivated crime? Using variation in travel distance to Texas abortion providers generated by post-HB-2 (2013) clinic closures, we show that reduced access causes lower labor force participation, rising debt, widening income inequality, and housing insecurity. This financial strain translates into higher rates of theft and burglary, with no detectable effect on violent crime.

</details>

<div class="actions">
[NBER working paper](https://www.nber.org/papers/w34245) ·
[SSRN](https://ssrn.com/abstract=5487070)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[The Effects of Switching Electronic Health Record Developer on Specialty Referrals]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag} NBER Working Paper 33861]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

When a hospital switches electronic health record (EHR) developer, how do its affiliated primary care physicians reroute specialty referrals? Using a difference-in-differences design across EHR switches, we find referrals to same-developer specialists rise 5.8% and referrals to specialists on the prior EHR fall 4.2%. A theoretical model shows PCP preferences for convenience can route patients away from the highest-quality specialists and fuel EHR-market agglomeration — an economic rationale for antitrust regulation of information blocking.

</details>

<div class="actions">
[NBER working paper](https://www.nber.org/papers/w33861) ·
[SSRN](https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5278364)
</div>
:::

::: {.paper}
[From Overdose Prevention to Reproductive Health: The Impact of Naloxone Access Laws]{.title}

[with Dajung Jun]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do opioid-overdose prevention policies spill over into reproductive health? We study pharmacy-access naloxone laws (PA-NALs), which allow non-patient-specific prescribing and expand pharmacist prescriptive authority. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design on U.S. birth records, we find PA-NALs raise fertility rates by 14%, cut low birth weight by 27%, and cut preterm birth by 10%, with effects amplifying over time. Reductions in low birth weight are most pronounced among mothers aged 25–29, non-White mothers, and first births.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Unlocking Creativity: Public Health Insurance Provision and Artists' Labor Market Outcomes]{.title}

[with Kevin S. Smith]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do artists need public health insurance to stabilize their careers? Using ACA Medicaid expansion and a staggered difference-in-differences design on 2009–2019 ACS data, we find expansion raised insurance coverage among artists by 2.2% and annual earnings by 3.5%. Artists shift away from self-employment and toward full-time work. Causal-forest and subgroup analyses place the gains among married and white artists. The results are consistent with a work-preference model in which relaxing income and health constraints expands the capacity to supply stable artistic labor.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[The Impact of E-Cigarette Regulations on Birth Outcomes]{.title}

[with Yizhao Wang]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do e-cigarette regulations improve infant health? We evaluate four policy tools — taxes, retail licensure, indoor vaping restrictions, and minimum legal sales age laws — using U.S. birth records and quarterly legislation data, 2005–2019. To separate each regulation from concurrent reforms, we develop a first-policy clean-window design. We find no statistically or economically meaningful effect on preterm birth, low birth weight, very low birth weight, or infant mortality. Null results hold across maternal age, race/ethnicity, and education. The mechanism is a weak behavioral first stage: most policies do not shift prenatal smoking, and those that do shift it too little to move population-level birth outcomes.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Design on Top of Design]{.title}

[single-authored]{.authors}

[[Under review]{.tag}]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

We study empirical design composition — layering one identification strategy on top of another — in linear models. A broad class of strategies shares a covariance condition for identification that is closed under three design moves: changing the identifying variation, transforming the residual, and changing the distribution. Closure makes design recursive: the same bias-diagnostic reasoning applies across strategies, before and after design moves. We develop a sequential test for whether each move reduces bias, and apply it to composed designs to visualize the full bias-correction path.

</details>
:::

## Working Papers

::: {.paper}
[Naloxone Coprescription Laws: Expanding Safety Without Fueling Misuse]{.title}

[with Shin-Yi Chou and Jiarun Li]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Can coprescription mandates expand naloxone access without fueling opioid misuse? Eighteen U.S. states now require physicians to prescribe or offer naloxone alongside high-risk opioids. Using Medicare Part D data (2013–2023) and a staggered difference-in-differences design, we find coprescription laws raise naloxone prescribing by 275%, with effects persisting at least five years. Laws that mandate coprescription produce twice the effect of laws that mandate only discussion. We find no evidence of increased opioid or benzodiazepine prescribing. Prescribing of opioid use disorder medications and treatment engagement both rise. Prescriber-focused harm reduction can expand treatment without fueling misuse.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Pressure from Students' Peer Parents]{.title}

[with Dapeng Chen and Junjian Yi]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Does the parenting style of a student's classroom peers affect the student? Exploiting random classroom assignments, we find that students surrounded by more authoritative or authoritarian peer parents experience significant deterioration in academic performance and mental health — especially female students, non-only children, and ethnic minorities. The mechanism is a worsened classroom environment driven by grade competition and shifts in teacher attention, intensified by competition among parents. Effects attenuate in schools that emphasize non-grade outcomes and invest in recreational amenities.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Gender Gap in Response to Work Environment Changes: The Impact of EHR Switching on Physician Referral Networks]{.title}

[single-authored]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Do male and female physicians adapt differently to technological disruption at work? Using a national primary-care referral panel, I measure how switching electronic health record developers reshapes referral networks. Modeling the gender gap as treatment-effect heterogeneity, I estimate responses with double machine learning causal forests. Men maintain more professional connections and adapt to new EHR systems more readily than women — especially among less-experienced physicians and those at smaller hospitals. The gap diminishes, and reverses, in the top deciles of experience and hospital size.

</details>
:::

::: {.paper}
[Habit Formation in the Consumption of Healthcare Services]{.title}

[with Chad D. Meyerhoefer]{.authors}

[Working paper, 2026.]{.venue}

<details class="paper-abstract">
<summary>Abstract</summary>

Does healthcare consumption exhibit habit formation? Extending the Grossman model of health capital, we show habitual preferences imply intertemporal dependency in utilization and damp demand response to price. Using Health and Retirement Study panel data, we bound Arellano-Bond estimates between the known biases of OLS and fixed-effect estimators. We find intertemporal dependency in nursing home nights, prescription drugs, and dental visits, but not several other services. RAND Health Insurance Experiment data rule out supplier-induced demand. Exploiting the price change induced by Medicare enrollment at age 65, we show demand for these services responds slowly to price. Policies that nudge consumption may have long-run effects on healthcare use.

</details>
:::
